retrieved_docs.title: Bulimia Nervosa -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Anorexia nervosa- It is essential to distinguish bulimia nervosa from anorexia nervosa because the complications, management, and prognosis of each condition are different. The patient has anorexia nervosa and not bulimia nervosa if binging or purging occurs exclusively during episodes of anorexia nervosa, which includes a binge-eating/purging type. An important distinction is that a diagnosis of anorexia nervosa requires low body weight; BMI<18, whereas this is not a diagnostic criterion for bulimia nervosa. Studies have shown minimal benefits of psychotropic medication for the treatment of anorexia nervosa.

retrieved_docs.content: The disorder generally starts in late adolescence or early adulthood.

retrieved_docs.title: Binge Eating Disorder -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Compared to individuals without eating disorders, those with binge eating disorders are statistically less likely to have a body mass index (BMI) <25 and are more likely to have a BMI ≥25. However, the precise distribution of these differences varies for each population tested.

retrieved_docs.title: Binge Eating Disorder -- Evaluation -- Psychiatric Screening for Binge Eating Disorder
retrieved_docs.content: Several screening questionnaires are used to assess for various eating disorders. Questionnaires used to evaluate binge eating disorders include: Binge eating scale [16] Three-factor eating questionnaire [17] Body shape questionnaire [18] Eating disorders examination [19] Structured clinical interview for the diagnosis of DSM disorders Questionnaire of eating and weight patterns [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Eating Disorders -- History and Physical -- Bulimia Nervosa
retrieved_docs.content: Individuals with bulimia nervosa may be underweight, normal weight, or slightly overweight. Even those who lost weight initially tend to regain and become overweight later gradually.

retrieved_docs.title: Bulimia Nervosa -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: GERD - gastroesophageal reflux disease, which can increase the risk of Barrett’s esophagus. [20] [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Eating Disorders -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The prognosis for persons with eating disorders is variable. The long-term prognosis is better with Bulimia nervosa when compared to anorexia nervosa. The binge eating and purging behaviors, duration of more than six years, lower body mass index, low motivation, unstable personality, concurrent depression, higher body image concerns, and dysfunctional relationships are consistently associated with poor treatment outcomes in all eating disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2900
retrieved_docs.content: CONDITIONS OF FETAL GROWTH AND MALNUTRITION represent a collective group of perinatal problems that encompass short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation. Short gestation, defined as delivery before full term, often results in low birth weight because the infant has had insufficient time for normal growth. Low birth weight, in turn, frequently reflects fetal growth retardation, a condition in which the fetus does not achieve its genetically expected size. These three entities are closely interrelated, each contributing to the broader spectrum of fetal growth and malnutrition disorders, and together they increase the risk of adverse neonatal outcomes. Effective maternal care that optimizes nutrition and prevents premature delivery is essential for reducing the incidence of these interconnected conditions.

retrieved_docs.content: Nutritional deficiencies, referred to in Arabic as نقص التغذية, represent a parent category that encompasses a wide range of specific deficiency conditions. This overarching concept includes various forms of inadequate intake or absorption of essential nutrients and serves as the broader classification under which individual deficiency disorders are organized. Recognizing nutritional deficiencies as the parent concept helps clinicians systematically assess and address the diverse manifestations of micronutrient and macronutrient insufficiencies in patient care.

retrieved_docs.content: Glucagon-like peptide‑1 (GLP‑1) analogues constitute a class of therapeutic agents that mimic the incretin hormone GLP‑1, enhancing glucose‑dependent insulin secretion, inhibiting glucagon release, slowing gastric emptying, and promoting satiety. Liraglutide is a member of this GLP‑1 analogue class, and its pharmacologic actions make it valuable for the treatment of type 2 diabetes mellitus and for weight management in patients with obesity.

retrieved_docs.content: Peripherally acting antiobesity products are classified as a specific type of antiobesity preparations, excluding diet products. This subclass of antiobesity agents exerts its therapeutic effects through peripheral mechanisms rather than central pathways, distinguishing them from diet products and centrally acting weight‑loss medications. Recognizing that peripherally acting antiobesity products belong to the broader category of antiobesity preparations, but are not considered diet products, helps clinicians select appropriate pharmacologic options for obesity management based on mechanism of action and product classification.
